---
title: "Airway Smooth Muscle RNA-seq Analysis"
subtitle: "Differential gene expression in dexamethasone-treated human airway smooth muscle cells"
author: "William Guesdon"
date: last-modified
date-format: "MMMM D, YYYY"

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    embed-resources: true
    fig-align: center
    fig-width: 8
    fig-height: 5
    df-print: kable

execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false

knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    out.width: "100%"

bibliography: references.bib
---

```{r setup}
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(stringr)
library(knitr)

RESULTS_DIR <- "../results"
PLOTS_DIR   <- "../analysis/plots"
COUNTS_FILE <- file.path(RESULTS_DIR, "rnaseq/airway/star_salmon/salmon.merged.gene_counts.tsv")
DE_FILE     <- file.path(RESULTS_DIR, "differentialabundance/airway/tables/differential/dex_vs_untrt.deseq2.results.tsv")

counts <- read_tsv(COUNTS_FILE, show_col_types = FALSE)
de     <- read_tsv(DE_FILE, show_col_types = FALSE)

gene_map <- counts |> select(gene_id, gene_name)

de <- de |>
  left_join(gene_map, by = "gene_id") |>
  mutate(
    sig = case_when(
      is.na(padj) ~ "NS",
      padj >= 0.05 ~ "NS",
      log2FoldChange > 0 ~ "Up",
      log2FoldChange < 0 ~ "Down"
    )
  )

n_tested <- de |> filter(!is.na(padj)) |> nrow()
n_sig    <- de |> filter(sig != "NS") |> nrow()
n_up     <- de |> filter(sig == "Up") |> nrow()
n_down   <- de |> filter(sig == "Down") |> nrow()
```

## Background

Glucocorticoids such as dexamethasone are front-line treatments for asthma, acting through broad transcriptional reprogramming of airway cells. **Himes et al. (2014)** [@himes2014] used RNA-seq to characterise the transcriptional response of primary human airway smooth muscle (ASM) cells from four donors treated with dexamethasone, identifying CRISPLD2 as a novel glucocorticoid-responsive gene. This dataset (GEO: GSE52778) has become a standard reference for Bioconductor differential expression tutorials.

This report re-analyses a subsampled version of the dataset (1M read pairs per sample) using two nf-core pipelines: **nf-core/rnaseq** v3.14.0 for quantification [@ewels2020; @patro2017] and **nf-core/differentialabundance** v1.4.0 for differential expression with DESeq2 [@love2014].

## Data & Methods

### Dataset

```{r dataset-table}
meta <- tibble::tribble(
  ~Sample,          ~Condition,       ~Cell_line, ~SRA,
  "N61311_untrt",   "Untreated",      "N61311",   "SRR1039508",
  "N61311_dex",     "Dexamethasone",  "N61311",   "SRR1039509",
  "N052611_untrt",  "Untreated",      "N052611",  "SRR1039512",
  "N052611_dex",    "Dexamethasone",  "N052611",  "SRR1039513",
  "N080611_untrt",  "Untreated",      "N080611",  "SRR1039516",
  "N080611_dex",    "Dexamethasone",  "N080611",  "SRR1039517",
  "N061011_untrt",  "Untreated",      "N061011",  "SRR1039520",
  "N061011_dex",    "Dexamethasone",  "N061011",  "SRR1039521"
)
kable(meta, caption = "Airway smooth muscle RNA-seq samples (Himes et al. 2014). Four donors, paired untreated/dexamethasone design.")
```

### Pipeline

The analysis was conducted using two nf-core pipelines executed on **AWS Batch** with Spot instances (c5/m5 families) and S3 storage via Fusion FUSE mounting.

**Step 1 — Quantification: nf-core/rnaseq v3.14.0**

| Stage | Tool | Purpose |
|-------|------|---------|
| Read QC | FastQC | Per-read quality assessment |
| Trimming | fastp | Adapter removal, quality filtering |
| Quantification | Salmon | Transcript-level pseudo-alignment |
| Aggregation | tximeta | Gene-level count summarisation |
| QC report | MultiQC | Aggregate quality dashboard |

Salmon was run against the GENCODE v44 (GRCh38.p14) human transcriptome with `--gencode` to strip version suffixes from transcript IDs.

**Step 2 — Differential expression: nf-core/differentialabundance v1.4.0**

| Stage | Tool | Purpose |
|-------|------|---------|
| Normalisation | DESeq2 | Median-of-ratios size factor estimation |
| DE testing | DESeq2 Wald test | Log2 fold change and p-value per gene |
| Visualisation | DESeq2 / ggplot2 | Volcano, PCA, dispersion plots |
| Pathway analysis | g:Profiler2 | Functional enrichment (GO, KEGG, Reactome) |

The DESeq2 model used `~ cell_line + condition`, blocking on cell line (donor) to account for inter-individual variability and isolate the dexamethasone treatment effect.

## Results

### Sample clustering

```{r fig1, fig.cap="Principal component analysis of VST-normalised expression (top 500 most variable genes). Samples cluster primarily by cell line (PC1), with treatment condition separating along PC2."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(PLOTS_DIR, "fig1_pca.png"))
```

PCA reveals that the dominant source of variation (PC1) is donor identity (cell line), consistent with the paired experimental design. The treatment effect (dexamethasone vs untreated) separates along PC2, confirming a consistent transcriptional response across all four donors.

```{r fig4, fig.cap="Sample-to-sample Euclidean distance heatmap (top 500 most variable genes). Paired samples from the same donor cluster together, validating the paired blocking design."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(PLOTS_DIR, "fig4_sample_distance.png"))
```

The sample distance heatmap confirms that paired samples from the same cell line are most similar, supporting the use of cell line as a blocking factor in the DESeq2 model.

### Differential expression

```{r fig2, fig.cap="Volcano plot of differential expression results. Red: upregulated (padj < 0.05, positive LFC); blue: downregulated. Top 15 most significant genes are labelled."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(PLOTS_DIR, "fig2_volcano.png"))
```

```{r de-summary}
tibble::tribble(
  ~Metric,                       ~Value,
  "Genes tested",                format(n_tested, big.mark = ","),
  "Significant (padj < 0.05)",   format(n_sig, big.mark = ","),
  "Upregulated",                 format(n_up, big.mark = ","),
  "Downregulated",               format(n_down, big.mark = ",")
) |>
  kable(caption = "Differential expression summary (DESeq2, dexamethasone vs untreated).")
```

```{r top-genes-table}
de |>
  filter(sig != "NS") |>
  arrange(padj) |>
  slice_head(n = 15) |>
  mutate(
    baseMean = round(baseMean, 1),
    log2FoldChange = round(log2FoldChange, 2),
    padj = formatC(padj, format = "e", digits = 2)
  ) |>
  select(Gene = gene_name, `Base mean` = baseMean,
         `log2 FC` = log2FoldChange, `Adj. p-value` = padj,
         Direction = sig) |>
  kable(caption = "Top 15 differentially expressed genes by adjusted p-value.")
```

Among the top upregulated genes, **FKBP5** (FK506-binding protein 5) and **TSC22D3** (GILZ) are well-known glucocorticoid-responsive transcription targets involved in anti-inflammatory signalling. **KLF15** and **ZBTB16** (PLZF) are transcription factors that mediate downstream glucocorticoid effects. **GPX3** (glutathione peroxidase 3) suggests an oxidative-stress response component.

Top downregulated genes include **CXCL12** (a chemokine suppressed by glucocorticoids), **KCTD12** (potassium channel tetramerisation domain), and **SOX4** (a transcription factor involved in developmental pathways).

### Top differentially expressed genes

```{r fig5, fig.cap="Top 10 up- and down-regulated genes ranked by adjusted p-value. Bar length indicates log2 fold change magnitude."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(PLOTS_DIR, "fig5_top_genes.png"))
```

```{r fig3, fig.cap="Heatmap of the 30 most significant differentially expressed genes (z-scored VST values). Rows are clustered by expression similarity; columns by sample. Clear separation between treated and untreated conditions is visible."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(PLOTS_DIR, "fig3_heatmap.png"))
```

The heatmap shows a clear and consistent expression pattern across all four donors: the top DE genes respond uniformly to dexamethasone treatment, with upregulated genes (red) clustering together in treated samples and downregulated genes (blue) in untreated samples.

### Expression of key genes

```{r fig6, fig.cap="Normalised expression counts for selected differentially expressed genes. Top row: upregulated genes (glucocorticoid-responsive); bottom row: downregulated genes."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(PLOTS_DIR, "fig6_gene_expression.png"))
```

Individual gene expression plots confirm robust and reproducible treatment effects across all four donors:

- **FKBP5** and **TSC22D3** (GILZ) show strong, consistent upregulation — these are canonical glucocorticoid response genes.
- **KLF15** and **ZBTB16** are transcription factors with pronounced dexamethasone-induced expression.
- **CXCL12** and **KCTD12** are consistently suppressed by treatment.
- **VCAM1** and **SOX4** show clear downregulation across all donors.

## Discussion

This analysis demonstrates a complete RNA-seq workflow — from raw FASTQ to differential expression and pathway analysis — applied to a well-characterised glucocorticoid response dataset. Key observations:

1. **The paired design is essential.** PCA and sample distance analysis show that inter-donor variation dominates the expression landscape. Blocking on cell line in the DESeq2 model correctly isolates the treatment effect from donor-specific baseline differences.

2. **Dexamethasone drives a broad transcriptional response.** `r n_sig` genes reach significance at padj < 0.05, with `r n_up` upregulated and `r n_down` downregulated — consistent with the known pleiotropic effects of glucocorticoid receptor activation.

3. **Top hits are biologically coherent.** The most significant genes (FKBP5, TSC22D3/GILZ, KLF15, ZBTB16) are established glucocorticoid targets, validating the analytical pipeline. Downregulated genes include chemokines (CXCL12) and developmental transcription factors (SOX4).

4. **Cloud execution is cost-effective.** Both pipelines completed in under 80 minutes total on AWS Batch Spot instances at a total cost of ~$0.11, demonstrating that production-grade bioinformatics workflows can run at minimal cost using spot pricing.

### Limitations

- **Subsampled data:** each sample was limited to 1M read pairs (original dataset has substantially deeper coverage), which may reduce sensitivity for low-expression genes.
- **Single contrast:** only dexamethasone vs untreated was tested; the original study examined additional time points and concentrations.
- **No independent validation:** results are consistent with published findings but were not validated with orthogonal methods (qPCR, protein-level).

## Reproducibility

All analyses were performed with publicly available tools. The complete workflow is available at this project's repository.

| Component | Version |
|-----------|---------|
| nf-core/rnaseq | 3.14.0 |
| nf-core/differentialabundance | 1.4.0 |
| Salmon | 1.10.x |
| DESeq2 | 1.42.x |
| g:Profiler2 | latest |
| R | 4.4.0 |
| Reference | GENCODE v44 (GRCh38.p14) |
| AWS Batch | Spot; c5/m5; Fusion FUSE |

```{r session-info}
sessionInfo()
```

## References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
